title: That of God in Every Person
summary: Interviewed by David Cline at their home in August 2013, with John Bishop behind the camera, Jean Rosenberg opens by tracing her start: born in Philadelphia and adopted by a transplanted New York couple whose father left a Presbyterian community for the Quakers, leading the family into the Abington meeting, intense social action, decision by consensus, and the Philadelphia yearly meeting's work on racial and peace concerns that guided her to Earlham College.

title: Sit in Front of the Plate Glass Window
summary: Jean recounts refusing to take part in air-raid drills at Abington High School as a psychology of fear, being called a Communist dupe by the principal and told to sit in front of the plate glass window to be killed first, and arranging to screen the film Operation Correction so students could see another side, protected somewhat by her standing as an honor student.

title: Have You Ever Thought About Joining Me
summary: Jean describes her awareness of the Vietnam War and her witness against it, and how her Earlham friend Dorothy, daughter of an internal revenue lawyer, had interned with John Doar indexing FBI reports on Klan members; when Dorothy had Jean introduce Doar at a convocation, he invited Jean over lunch to join the Civil Rights Division, and she was accepted as a research analyst at the Department of Justice in June 1965.

title: A School for Jewish Children in Magdeburg
summary: John Rosenberg introduces himself as Jean's husband of forty-six years, born in Magdeburg, Germany in 1931, where his father, a teacher, set up a school for Jewish children after Hitler required the schools be segregated, also working for the Jewish welfare agency and assisting the rabbi, the family living in a building next to a synagogue.

title: The Last Ship
topic: Escape and Settling in the Carolinas
summary: John tells of Kristallnacht and the bombing of the synagogue, his father's eleven days in a concentration camp where he taught by the Socratic method, the family's year in Holland, and their arrival in the United States on February 22, 1940 on nearly the last ship, while a brother, sisters with five children in Rotterdam, and his grandmother were lost; in America his father swept mill floors while writing sermons for Jewish communities in Spartanburg and Gastonia before John grew up an Eagle Scout in Gastonia and went off to Duke.

title: They Took the Binoculars
summary: John remembers Treasury agents confiscating his immigrant family's binoculars and shortwave radio during the war, embarrassed to find Franklin Roosevelt's picture on the patriotic family's wall, and describes Gastonia as a segregated society with Black schools and a comfortable Jewish merchant community that respected his learned father but, grateful simply to be there, took no initiative against segregation.

title: Everybody Knows Where the Lines Are
summary: John reflects on Gastonia's totally segregated schools and his mother, a friendly saleswoman who offered Black and white customers alike something to eat, recalls a county voting rights case that reached the Supreme Court and a much later joint class reunion of the 1948 Black and white graduates, and explains how a Gastonia trustee secured him a Duke tuition scholarship that ended when the man died after his first year.

part: From the Service to the Division
title: He Went to the Back of the Train
summary: John tells of becoming a chemistry major and ROTC cadet at Duke, then an Air Force navigator flying Cold War exercises near the Russian border, and the life-changing moment when he and a radar operator from Charleston named Abe Jenkins flew a plane back from Europe, only for Jenkins, in uniform in Washington, to head for the back of the train where the Black passengers rode, leaving John thinking this was really wrong and that someday he might do something about it.

title: You Depend on Each Other
summary: John describes a relatively integrated Air Force still carrying a fair amount of racism, joking, and a Confederate sticker among navigation-school roommates from Texas and New Orleans, and how flying dangerous crewed missions where survival depended on one another taught the Black and white officers, in everyone's first real experience of it, that the color of one's skin made no difference at all.

title: A Jewish Quota at Duke
summary: John recalls that Duke was still segregated with a Jewish quota, his two assigned Jewish roommates Norman and Marty (one a lifelong friend who became a lawyer in Jacksonville), and joining the then all-Jewish fraternity; Jean interjects that her own Quaker college in 1961 intentionally assigned her a Black roommate from the Bronx who became a lifelong friend, and John notes serving as a national delegate when the fraternity's charter was changed to admit non-Jewish members.

title: A Place I Could Make a Contribution
summary: John describes going to law school at Chapel Hill, befriending the brilliant student who became number one in their class, and his determination to join the Civil Rights Division, explaining that being born in Germany and seeing the Holocaust, then serving alongside Black servicemen, convinced him this country's caste system was wrong and that, perhaps as a southerner with an easier line of communication, he could make a contribution representing the United States in these cases.

title: Go Find Out What the Facts Are
summary: John recounts the Civil Rights Division, created in 1957 with little authority, where lawyers reviewed papers until John Doar arrived in 1960 with six months left in the Eisenhower administration and insisted on going south, working a Louisiana case where ginners refused to gin a registered voter's cotton, which led Robert Kennedy to keep Doar on to run the trial work; he explains the division's 1962 mission to challenge voting discrimination while protecting James Meredith's entry to Ole Miss took considerable time, and recalls a photograph of Burke Marshall and Doar with Meredith confronted by the lieutenant governor.

title: Chased Out of Town With a Pistol
summary: John describes being assigned first to Georgia amid a series of church burnings, working with a liberal lawyer who had lost his business in Fort Smith, Arkansas, and one of their very first cases against a deputy sheriff in Sasser, near Americus, who chased three or four Black students with a pistol after they went door to door registering voters, leading to a criminal information and a federal trial.

title: Calling Them Mister and Missus
summary: John recalls his first federal criminal prosecution where, the lead lawyer's plane delayed, he examined the Black student witnesses himself and addressed them by their last names as Mister and Missus, which southerners found unusual, while the lead lawyer still believed southerners could be persuaded to comply; he was then transferred to Mississippi at the end of 1962, and reflects on time spent in Albany, Georgia trying to gather information for the division with no success on the nighttime church burnings whose witnesses would have required a way into the white community or the Klan.

title: Getting Bob Moses Out of Jail
summary: John recounts how SNCC and the SCLC, primarily SNCC, launched a major registration effort around 1961 with Bob Moses given charge of Mississippi, how the division brought one of its first cases to stop a prosecution of Moses for resisting arrest, and how, after Moses and seven or eight SNCC members were jailed in Leflore County for encouraging people to register, the division sued the county officials and John helped negotiate getting Moses and the group out of the Greenwood jail.

title: If They Lived and Breathed They Could Vote
summary: John explains the field method of locating community leaders and Black residents who had tried to register, how registrars stalled them while whites could register over the phone or, as he used to say, if they lived and breathed they could vote, and how those who got in faced a literacy test on a section of the Constitution; he describes having the FBI photograph all the voting records to show in Washington that illiterate whites were passed while Black applicants were failed, with whites giving incomprehensible interpretations of provisions like the ban on imprisonment for debt.

title: The Freezing Principle
summary: John contrasts Judge Frank Johnson of Montgomery, a beacon who ruled in the Macon County case that the same standards frozen for whites had to apply to Black applicants and required protection for his strong orders, with Judge Cox of Jackson, an avowed segregationist and former roommate of Senator Eastland who threw up roadblocks, taking two or three years to even hear a motion to photograph records.

title: Assigned by Counties
summary: John explains the division's geographic assignments under section chiefs (one nearly blind lawyer from California, another over Mississippi from Texas, another over Louisiana), how he worked Leflore County and Greenwood plus Jackson and several other counties, and the running joke about Robert Kennedy asking the plan early on, when Burke Marshall said they needed just four more lawyers because Eastland chaired the judiciary committee and getting thirty was unthinkable.

title: Fannie Lou Hamer Off the Bus
summary: John and Jean describe how, until the Civil Rights Act, almost everything revolved around the registration effort, that the big expansion came after the 1964 act, and the incident with Fannie Lou Hamer of Ruleville, who with several other voter-registration workers returning from a workshop got off the bus at a lunch counter in Montgomery County and was arrested, taken to jail, and beaten for no reason.

title: I Mopped the Blood Off the Floor
topic: The Hamer Case and an Acquittal
summary: John tells how the division filed a criminal case against the officers who beat Mrs. Hamer and the workers, how the FBI claimed it could not find the jail trustee, and how John, working in Mississippi the day Ruby shot Oswald, found the trustee, who said he had mopped the blood off the jail floor and would testify; the all-white jury acquitted in about two hours, and John drove to Sunflower County to tell Mrs. Hamer they had lost, noting Black leaders still saw a difference between the FBI and the Justice lawyers and welcomed them, though what they wanted was federal protection John said the system would not provide.

title: He Shot Back at the Klan
summary: John describes Hartman Turnbow of Holmes County, who led people to register near a community of Black landowners outside Lexington, whose home was firebombed and shot into and who fired back at the Klansmen, only to be arrested for setting his own house afire, prompting a division case to free him that the courts again deferred to state proceedings, and notes the local editor Hazel Brannon Smith and a book chapter written about Turnbow.

title: My Name Is John Doar
topic: From the Evers Funeral to the Lynd Trial
summary: John recalls 1963 heating up as President Kennedy and Medgar Evers were assassinated, and how after the Evers funeral in Jackson, with mourners and police about to clash, John Doar walked into the street with his hand out declaring his name was John Doar of the Justice Department who stood for what was right, calming the crowd; he then returns to categorizing photographed records and an entire morning of articulate Black teachers turned down by a registrar near Hattiesburg held in contempt in the Lynd case, testimony that appalled the three appellate judges.

title: The Floor Theater in Greenwood
summary: John explains that the 1964 Civil Rights Act, promoted by Lyndon Johnson after Kennedy's death, made the division responsible for school desegregation, employment, and public accommodations, and tells how in Greenwood a national-chain theater complied and admitted Black patrons who were then beaten while police stood by, while the local Paramount refused outright, leading the division to sue both the police officers and the theater, cases eventually resolved in the division's favor.

title: This Was the Culture of the FBI
summary: Answering the interviewer on jurisdiction, John explains that the FBI was the Department of Justice's investigative agency under the attorney general, that until the civil rights statutes its work was bank robberies, kidnappings, and interstate crimes, and that enforcing civil rights was difficult for local white southern agents who believed in segregation, a problem the murder of the three civil rights workers brought into focus; he recounts the need for agents from elsewhere, his own jury analysis of an eighty-person pool, and the few courageous white editors such as Hazel Brannon Smith and Hodding Carter whose papers were bombed.
related_events: Murder of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

title: He Came Down From New York
summary: John describes the change toward non-southern agents, a key agent in the Meridian civil rights case who worked with informants, and the New York agent who led the investigation, recounting the long process of a grand jury that heard evidence of local police brutality but produced no indictment until two confessions led to the conviction of seven men while a Klan minister's jury hung, and how, when these federal cases were brought local law enforcement might as well not have been there, so that after the murders the division lawyers, who had driven alone, began driving in twos.
related_events: Murder of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

title: He Shot a .38 Into the Floor
summary: John tells the one real incident of trouble: in Yazoo County, with the FBI photographing voting records in the next room, the segregationist registrar nicknamed Foot wanted to chat, boasted of the lynchings he had witnessed, then pulled a .38 from his drawer and fired it into the floor in front of John's foot as a statement, bringing the FBI running, the only time anything like that happened to him in all those years.

part: The Voting Rights Act and Selma
title: If You Signed Your Name You Could Vote
topic: The Act Turns It Upside Down
summary: John explains that the Voting Rights Act of 1965 turned the whole voting episode right side up by abolishing literacy tests and allowing federal examiners into southern counties under a formula so that people could register simply by signing their names, and turns to Alabama and Sheriff Jim Clark of Selma, a poster child for segregationists who for over a year obstructed registration drives, used a cattle prod on students, and was challenged in a federal case John presented in December 1965.

title: They Impounded the Black Precincts
summary: John recounts the first 1966 Alabama primary under the Voting Rights Act, when registration surged from about seven thousand to fifteen thousand voters in Sheriff Jim Clark's county, and how on a seventy-three-item paper ballot the white election officials finished and impounded the boxes from five Black precincts that came in at four in the morning, then decided to exclude them as fraudulent, prompting the Civil Rights Division to sue to require the ballots be counted.

title: Counting Every One of Seventeen Thousand
summary: John describes filing the federal case, getting Judge Johnson to order the seventeen thousand ballots kept safe before the case was assigned to Judge Thomas, who had long resisted desegregating the Mobile schools, while Jean and Dorothy spent a week watching the ballot boxes in the sheriff's office, observed by deputies who tried to romance them and gave Dorothy a segregationist Never button.

title: The Right to Vote Was Too Important
summary: John recounts the several-day trial before Judge Thomas with hundreds of federal observers who had watched the count, his thought of putting Jean on the stand, and how Judge Thomas, despite his record of keeping schools segregated, found only minor irregularities and ruled that a voter should not be deprived of a counted vote, ordering the ballots counted, a big first win on the voting rights case.

title: Viola Liuzzo on the Road
topic: From the March to the Supreme Court
summary: John recalls the Viola Liuzzo killing after the Selma to Montgomery march, when a woman of conscience from near Detroit was shot by the Klan while driving a young Black man, and reflects that the principles of the 1965 act came out of those earlier cases; asked about the recent Supreme Court ruling, he and Jean say Justice Ginsburg and the minority were right, note Hillary Clinton at the American Bar Association urging it be turned around as states rolled out voter ID laws, and that, like Bush v. Gore, you work to change it rather than going into the streets.

title: Doused by the Local Barber
summary: John recalls roughly a month in Bogalusa, Louisiana, helping build a case around incidents against local Black residents testing restaurants and public accommodations, much of it FBI work, prompted in part by threats and leaflets that scared off a liberal senator from Arkansas from speaking, and references photographs of a barber dousing a protester and CORE marches led by James Farmer that the division lawyers, including John, observed alongside another attorney.

title: A Judge in His Undershirt
summary: John explains the history of equity, that one could see the chancellor even in his nightshirt for relief, and tells how, to get a preliminary injunction against the Klan in the Bogalusa case, he found a Fifth Circuit judge in his back office on a sweltering Saturday in New Orleans, sitting in his undershirt, who signed the order, so that John likes to say he got an injunction from the judge in his undershirt.

title: Your Feet Feel Like Mud
summary: Jean, not yet married to John in those Alabama years, recounts the real fear of the work, water spit in their drinks and never knowing if it would escalate, and a night on the Meredith March when, serving as the traveling connector between the field attorneys and the attorney general, she and an attorney from Mississippi named George took a late walk in Yazoo City and a car screeched up with men jumping out to follow them, George telling her to look straight ahead and keep walking though her feet felt like mud.

title: A Field Office in Selma
summary: John and Jean recall the division opening its first field offices, the first in Selma led by a lawyer who later went to a Minneapolis firm and then divinity school, and mention the office's first Black secretary in Selma, leading Jean to a hard memory from preparing a massive Alabama school desegregation case with that secretary as her roommate during three days and nights around the clock.

title: They Saw She Was Black and Wouldn't Take Her
topic: A Seizure and a Refused Ambulance
summary: Jean tells of her roommate, the Black secretary from Selma, who after the three-day school case had run out of epilepsy medication and suffered a grand mal seizure in their Montgomery motel, how the first ambulance crew saw she was Black and refused to take her until John helped get another, how the hospital sent her home only for another seizure to follow, a hard lesson in how unequal things were that, John adds, made him figure they might as well get married.

title: I Think I'm Going to Ask Jean to Marry Me
summary: Jean and John recount that after that trip she flew home to Philadelphia while John was ordered to Charlotte to investigate the Klan bombing of attorney Julius Chambers's house, near John's family in Gastonia, and how, standing in a lunch line with the colleague helping him, John said he was going to call Jean and ask her to marry him, then proposed over the phone, after which her parents drove to meet John's parents the next day.

title: You Knew You Were Living History
summary: Asked how they kept going, John and Jean say everyone in the division, from John Doar to the secretaries, did the very best they could because they knew this was history, that it was hard on the wives at home and brought some divorces, and that against segregationist judges like Cox the work meant making a record so the next court would reverse them; John reflects the cases were the first attack and first law, that Bob Moses knew he could call any hour, and that knowing local people like Fannie Lou Hamer chopping cotton or a father who only wanted his child in school, putting their lives on the line, was enough to keep them going.

title: From Fifteen to Eighty Years Old
topic: A Church Group Jailed in Itta Bena
summary: John recalls that in rural counties people took the Justice Department lawyers in suits for insurance salesmen, and tells of a case in the Black section of Itta Bena outside Greenwood where, after a smoke bomb was thrown into a church the group had filled night after night, the whole group from age fifteen to eighty went to the deputy sheriff's house, were arrested for disturbing the peace, tried in groups of ten, fined and sentenced to thirty days at the county farm, where John interviewed them daily for two weeks until church bond money freed them.

title: It Was Really a Work Day
topic: The March on Washington and Resurrection City
summary: Jean recalls being in the Department of Justice on the first floor during the March on Washington, stepping outside to watch though it was a work day while she handled the Hamer case, and she and John remember a later observer role around Resurrection City and the Poor People's Campaign, when Marian Wright Edelman negotiated with John Doar over how long the encampment could stay to press its demands on voting.

title: Extradition Papers for James Earl Ray
summary: John recounts the Washington riots after Martin Luther King's assassination in 1968 and the Nixon inauguration, and how he and a senior Justice Department official went to Memphis to prepare the extradition papers to bring James Earl Ray back from England, gathering affidavits from police and fingerprint experts and examining the gun, the case assembled in Washington but the information gathered by the division, and recalls just visiting the civil rights museum there.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: A Brochure With Wallace's Picture
summary: John explains the poll tax as another Reconstruction-era device, cumulative and only in the South, which the Voting Rights Act required the attorney general to contest forthwith, so that on the Monday after the act was signed he filed the Mississippi poll tax case before preparing the Alabama case argued by a colleague, and how that record captured a brochure Governor Wallace had the highway patrol distribute through the white schools and a legislative history of frank remarks from 1900 about stopping Black citizens from voting, leading the court to invalidate the poll tax.

title: A Blessing to Have Them on the Court
topic: Fred Gray and the Fifth Circuit Judges
summary: John recounts a Montgomery election case brought by the prominent Black attorney Fred Gray, in which Judge Johnson ordered the United States made a party to keep the division's lawyers in his court, a case of no particularly good outcome beyond some election-process reforms, and praises the Fifth Circuit judges, naming Wisdom, Brown, and Bell, a blessing on the court when so many southern district judges made litigation slow and difficult, the trial records they built ultimately making the record for the Voting Rights Act.

title: The Briefcases Lined Up on Friday
summary: John describes living in Washington while gone much of the time, the briefcases lined up outside the division offices on Fridays as lawyers caught DC-3 flights to Memphis or Birmingham for two weeks to photograph four or five counties of voting records, including thousands in the Jackson and Rankin courthouses, and how in larger cities some Black residents had registered but not by carte blanche, while the line lawyers grew up under a disciplined complaint-drafting system.

title: Dot Maps of Houston
summary: John and Jean recall moving to Houston for six months for the long-running school case under Judge Connally, color-coding dot maps of every student residence (Black in red, white in green) to show how children were bused past schools, an antitrust-scale effort with foot lockers of records seeking busing to remedy past discrimination amid a Black core ringed by Spanish-speaking and white neighborhoods, which the judge addressed by pairing schools before the appeals court later required some busing.

title: They Threw the School Board Out
summary: John reflects that the best outcome in Houston was voters throwing out the school board and the lawyer who had made a living resisting desegregation, as in Little Rock and other districts, and turns to employment cases brought as chief of the Western section, suing the electrical workers in Las Vegas, the steam fitters in Los Angeles, and Roadway Express, and the division's expansion into schools in Indianapolis and Oklahoma City because segregation, often de facto, was not only southern.

title: Which Congressional District
summary: John explains leaving the division because the administration changed and which congressional district a case fell in seemed to matter more than its merits, prompting some lawyer revolts over weak enforcement, and the difficult question of whether a committed person should stay and fight or whether staying in government slowly priced one out, after which he and Jean, deciding money was not all that important, took a three-month camping trip to Canada and the national parks with a six-month-old before formally leaving.

title: Night Comes to the Cumberlands
summary: John recounts how a former colleague directing legal services for the Office of Economic Opportunity offered to support public-interest work in Appalachia, how they camped at a Kentucky state park on their way to visit his parents in Florida, and how they interviewed Harry Caudill of Whitesburg, author of Night Comes to the Cumberlands, about the broad form deed, strip mining, black lung, and the poverty issues facing a region with few lawyers free of the coal companies.

title: A First-Class Law Firm for Poor People
summary: John describes coming to Prestonsburg to build the Appalachian legal services program covering the eastern Kentucky coal counties, the strip-mining and black lung environmental issues, and the poverty issues of food stamps and Medicaid where local welfare offices treated applicants badly, his aim being a first-class law firm for poor people, the way John Doar had assembled good lawyers to represent the government.

title: They Thought We Were Communists
summary: John and Jean recount the opposition when federal legal-services funding arrived: the local bar fearing lost money, the community suspecting them of being communists or outside agitators, a Supreme Court case involving the Appalachian Volunteers who had lived with local families and encouraged them to seek benefits, and hospitals turning away people without money, including pregnant mothers, so that a lawyer could make a big difference for people with disability, consumer, and foreclosure problems.

title: Eleven Offices Across the Mountains
summary: John describes expanding the program to eleven offices, this one the main administrative office, and starting a separate nonprofit, the Appalachian Citizens Law Center, on the coal issues when he retired in 2002, with each office covering several counties and aiming for four lawyers to handle day-to-day divorces and custody alongside larger cases such as suing the state park system for discriminating against Black applicants, hoping to build a first-class law firm for poor people in remote eastern Kentucky.

title: You Can Mine Coal Responsibly
summary: John and Jean reflect that unlike the South, where they prepared cases and went home to Washington, here they became part of the local community, caring about its schools and environment, and discuss the difficulty of arguing for environmental regulation when coal mining is people's livelihood, that coal can be mined responsibly, and that for years out-of-state interests owned the coal and paid no taxes on it until that was changed.

title: A Planetarium in the Mountains
topic: A Science Center and a Closing Reflection
summary: John and Jean describe the present hardship as coal miners lose jobs with no replacement industries, and their effort, inspired by the natural history museum in John's hometown of Gastonia, to start a local science center with a planetarium and classroom space to lift low math and science scores, secured politically over a couple of legislatures because the governor and speaker were from neighboring counties; closing the interview, they reflect that you feel very fortunate when your values are congruent with what you do day to day, and that they have been able to do that.
